data.title: Circle Internet: Anticipating Stablecoin Market Growing 8x; Initiate With 'Buy'
data.description:
Circle Internet (NYSE:CRCL) launched its USDC, the stablecoin network in 2018, with nearly $6 trillion in on-chain transactions in 2025. The company’s stablecoins have gained market adoption among many payments and enterprise companies. I am initiating with a ‘Buy’ rating with a fair value of $147 per share.

Circle Internet successfully raised $1.05 billion in recent IPO in June 2025. The company issues two payment stablecoins: USDC for US dollars and EURC for EURO currency. I think the Circle technology’s advantages can be summarized as follows:

  • Stablecoins are highly liquid and can be redeemed for the underlying fiat currency. As such, these digital currencies could provide a crucial bridge between blockchain and traditional currency, as noted in its IPO prospectus. Unlike traditional finance, stablecoins can be easily transferred from one account to another, leading many payment companies to use stablecoins for daily transactions, remittances and trading purposes.
  • USDC has gradually gained market adoptions over the past few years. As detailed in the table below, USDC is the second largest stablecoin by the total market cap. According to Circle Internet, its USDC processes around 29% of total market share. The management has prioritized the market adoption of USDC and EURC through heavy investments in platform innovations.
  • Stablecoins are highly transparent and can satisfy the compliance and regulatory requirements, as these digital currencies are fully backed by currency reserves. Enterprises holding these stablecoins are less likely to encounter regulatory issues during accounting audits. In other words, CFOs are not risking their jobs to adopt stablecoins for their daily accounting activities.
  • Lastly, Circle technology provides other liquidity services such as institutional minting, reserving, redemption and FX for its stablecoins. These additional services could enable institutional customers to fully leverage Circle technology’s platform and technology for implementing blockchain networks.

I think Circle technology is still in the early stages of penetrating the huge market of cross-border payments, decentralized finance and e-commerce solutions. It is evident that stablecoins offer several advantages in terms of transparency, transaction fees and fast settlement. These benefits could position stablecoins for digital payments, cross-border transactions as well as other services such as e-commerce and merchant solutions, in my view.

According to CoinDesk, the stablecoin market was equivalent to only 1% of US M2 money supply and 1% of total FX transactions in 2024, with potential growing to 10% of U.S. money supply and FX transactions. That means the overall market could potentially grow tenfold from the current size, which could lead to tremendous business growth for Circle technology.

To be conservative, I assume stablecoin will reach 8% of money supply and FX transactions in a 10-year frame.timeframe. As such, I calculate the overall market will grow at a CAGR of 23%. I anticipate Circle technology will grow by 25% annually in revenue, slightly higher than the overall market growth driven by its scale and market share gains.

It’s worth noting that Circle technology is a profitable business excluding some one-off costs, as detailed in the table below. They achieved a 9.7% of operating margin with $344 million in cash from operating activities.

I forecast 200bps annual margin expansion, driven by 100bps from gross profits improvements, 50bps from lower marketing expenses and 50bps from operating leverage of total compensations. Circle technology has spent heavily on compensations, G&A and sales/marketing when they launched the USDC and EURC products. As the company scales, I forecast the overall expenses will gradually decline as a percentage of total revenue. I calculate the total operating costs will grow by 22% annually, leading to 200bps margin expansion.

With these assumptions, DCF can be summarized as follows:

FCFF:

WACC = 11.4% assuming risk free 4.2%; beta 1.2; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $23 bn; debt $40 Mn; tax rate 20%. I set the terminal growth rate to be 5% aligned with the overall financial market growth.

Discounting all the FCFF and adjusting net cash, the fair value is calculated to be $147 per share, as detailed below:

Key Risks

  • While Circle technology has been growing rapidly, Tether remains the market leader in the stablecoin market, benefiti from first-mover advantages and a strong presence in emerging markets. As reported by the media, Tether considers to build its own U.S. payment network for stablecoins, creating a new payment token specifically for US market. Considering Tether’s size, Circle technology might face challenges in catching up with its rival.
  • Circle technology held $31 million in digital assets on its balance sheet in FY24. The digital assets are highly volatile in terms of valuation; as such, their operational performance might be adversely affected if the price of its digital assets declines. For instance, the company incurred a $57 million loss in FY22 due to price fluctuations.
  • Lastly, the stablecoin legislations are underway, as reported by the media. Specifically, the GENIUS Act and STABLE Act will implement regulations on stablecoin issuers, in terms of capital reserve, redemption, management, and safekeeping. In addition, the bill allows foreign issuers of stablecoins to issue in the US market, which could potentially increase the competitive landscape.

Conclusion

As the second largest player, Circle technology is well-positioned to benefit from the rapid adoption of stablecoins. I am initiating with a ‘Buy’ rating with a fair value of $147 per share.

data.title: Two Cheers (And A Warning) For May's Inflation Slowdown
data.description:
By Alexander W. Salter

Central bankers are in a pickle. But the current, frustrating tradeoffs result from short-sighted fiscal policies of the past.

Disinflation is no longer a blip, but a trend. The Bureau of Labor Statistics reported that the Consumer Price Index (CPI) increased 0.1 percent in May, down from 0.2 percent in April. The annual rate of change of 2.4 percent was almost identical to April’s and slightly lower than March’s.

Core inflation, which excludes volatile food and energy prices, also rose 0.1 percent last month. It has risen 2.8 percent over the past year.

As with previous months, shelter prices pulled up the average. “The index for shelter rose 0.3 percent in May and was the primary factor in the all items monthly increase,” the BLS noted. Much (if not all) of the excess shelter inflation can be attributed to measurement error: the shelter index lags market rents. As a result, the index for shelter tends to overestimate the rise in actual shelter prices during the later phase of a tightening cycle.

Ongoing disinflation presents a challenge for central bankers. Monetary policy passively tightens when inflation slows down. That’s because a given market (nominal) rate of interest corresponds to a higher real (inflation-adjusted) rate of interest when prices rise less quickly.

The current target range for the federal funds rate is 4.25 to 4.50 percent. Adjusting for inflation, using the 12-month headline inflation figure yields a real interest rate range of 1.85 to 2.10 percent. If we use the annualized three-month figure (0.8 percent) instead, the range becomes 3.45 to 3.70 percent.

Fed estimates for the natural rate of interest — the real interest rate that equilibrates supply and demand in short-term capital markets — suggest monetary policy is currently tight. The New York Fed estimates the natural rate of interest was between 0.78 and 1.37 percent in 2025:Q1. The Richmond Fed’s median estimate for the same quarter is 1.76 percent. Inflation-adjusted market rates are higher than these estimates using the year-over-year price data, and much higher than these estimates using the annualized three-month data. This certainly looks like restrictive monetary conditions.

We must also consult monetary data. If the money supply is growing faster than money demand, monetary policy is loose; if slower, tight.

The M2 money supply is growing 4.45 percent per year. Broader measures of the money supply, which are liquidity-weighted, are growing between 3.99 and 4.03 percent per year. Money demand, which we estimate by adding population growth, 1 percent according to the most recent census figures to real GDP growth (2.06 percent in 2025:Q1), is growing about 3.06 percent per year. It looks like monetary policy is loose because the money supply is growing faster than money demand.

But remember, the first quarter’s GDP figures were artificially low because of a one-time import spike. A better estimate would incorporate expectations for 2025:Q2. The Wall Street Journal’s average forecast is 0.8 percent next quarter, 0.6 percent in the third quarter, and 1.1 percent in the fourth quarter. Hence, it is likely the US economy is currently growing much faster than the 2025:Q1 figure would suggest. Higher real GDP growth means higher money demand growth. We are probably close to neutral, as measured by money supply and money demand.

The new CPI data reinforces recent Personal Consumption Price Index (PCEPI) data: inflation is falling, which makes monetary policy tighter. The Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) next meets June 17-18, but they are unlikely to loosen policy. FOMC members have indicated they will keep the fed funds target range where it is. Tight money will continue for a while longer.

Restrictive monetary policy is appropriate in some sense. Inflation has been higher than the Fed’s two-percent target for three years. Furthermore, there is a worrying trend that inflation is settling into the 2.25-2.50 percent range. That would be unacceptable. The Fed cannot permit a long-run inflation rate that exceeds its target between 12.5 and 25 percent without losing major credibility.

Yet, FOMC members must also worry about the opposite prospect: keeping money too tight for too long risks a recession. Prices, including those embedded in contracts, likely reflect the higher-than-average inflation rates we’ve experienced for the past three years. Should total spending on goods and services prove insufficient to justify those higher prices (due to excessive monetary tightening), the result might be reduced production and rising unemployment.

I would not want to be a central banker right now. Top Fed decision makers have some difficult times ahead. But they wouldn’t be in this situation if they hadn’t bowed to fiscal pressures during the coronavirus pandemic. Today’s frustrating policy tradeoffs reflect yesterday’s short-sighted deviation from sound policy. Until we change how the Fed works at a fundamental level, it will continue to find itself in pickles like this.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: PayPal: Just Like The Stock, NFC Integration Is Undervalued
data.description:
Introduction

Since I last covered PayPal Holdings Inc. (PYPL), shares have jumped by over 30% amid a broader market recovery following peak tariff fear lows. I rated it a Strong Buy, outlining its impeccable risk-reward ratio at compressed prices. Today, roughly two months later, I want to revisit PayPal and make an update to my rating.

In this article, I will focus on the NFC payment integration PayPal has recently rolled out. I believe it is a key addition to their all-in-one payments and finance ecosystem and makes them competitors to Apple Pay and credit card conglomerates like Visa (V) or Mastercard (MA) That said, PYPL's risk reward was a bit better in early April, leading me to downgrade the stock to "only" a Buy.

Developments

Since Q1 2025 earnings have already been out for a few weeks and a lot of fellow analysts have covered them in-depth, I only want to briefly touch on them. Revenue growth slowed to just 1% YoY at $7.79B. Analysts were expecting $60M more. They did beat on earnings, however, with non-GAAP EPS rising 23% YoY and reaching $1.33, far above expectations of $1.16. My preferred measure of profitable growth, transaction margin dollars excluding interest on customer balances, grew 7% YoY to $3.4B, which is solid but slightly less than I hoped for.

Let's now address the most important part of this article: NFC/in-store payments. Having been a PayPal customer for roughly five years and a shareholder for the past one and a half years, I've always asked myself why PayPal's usability was restricted primarily to online purchases, such as shopping at a clothing brand. While the digital payments market has and will witness impressive growth, I do believe that traditional retail sales are an important pillar of purchasing transactions and will not disappear anytime soon. Previously, PayPal missed this significant opportunity, allowing established financial giants Visa (V), Mastercard (MA), and American Express (AMEX) to dominate this market. Now, I am more than happy to observe a strategic shift, in which PayPal finally embraces these payment options by issuing their very own card. It works by subtracting money from your account or bank account balance. The fact that this new offering is a focus becomes clear when it is the first thing you can see on their homepage.

With over 430 million active users of PayPal wallet, there will be many millions of new users of this card. I truly believe that PayPal can make great use of their network effects here, which increases adoption and reduces cost of distribution. Further, this development reframes the competition angst surrounding PYPL. Essentially, they turned the situation around by now having become serious competitors to Apple Pay (and Google Pay), which were previously thought to be serious competitors for PayPal. Another key benefit derived from this is that the number of total transactions will grow rapidly. This will help PayPal collect and analyze more accurate data and thereby improve advertising results and agentic commerce experience. With offline payments now contributing to TPV, TPV growth could pick up again and reach a high single digit figure long term. This would help PayPal create much needed top-line growth again. Obviously, offline payments from a credit card built on a partnership with Mastercard will not achieve the highest of margins for the company, but should, over time, help to further contribute to expected bottom line growth. Personally, I have used PYPL's NFC feature more than 20 times already and it went seamlessly everywhere.

At today's prices, shares of PYPL change hands at 2.4X sales, 10.2X EBITDA and 16.7X earnings. These levels are low both historically and when comparing them to the S&P 500 or other stocks of similar quality. If any of the current innovations PayPal is working on contribute to the top and/or bottom line, I believe that these values are indeed too low and in need of a slight re-rating. Since the interim low on April 7, PYPL's P/S, EV/EBITDA, as well as their P/E ratio have all expanded by around 27%. That does make the company more expensive than it was two months ago. Their forward P/E, now at 14.7X, also went up by 27% (from 11.6X) as of my previous coverage. This increase in multiples is one key reason why I am downgrading PayPal.

When looking at technicals, PayPal no doubt looks rough on a longer-term chart. Zooming in, it found very strong support at the $57 mark, a level we are unlikely to lose. After its ramp up it had a brief pullback of around 7%, which bounced off of the 400 day moving average and a recently established support trend line starting from the April lows. Both these levels could support future pullbacks. Weekly RSI at 53 leads me to believe that the uptrend can continue. Specifically, my first price target is $82.7, the lower end of a large gap (only visible in the daily chart), while my second price target stands at $92, the key resistance price rejected from in December and January. With a daily RSI closing in on 70 and the S&P500 being due for at least a small pullback, though, I believe in the short term we might see a pullback in PYPL stock as well. That would be a good time to add.

Risks

Risks for PayPal have not changed drastically since my last coverage. They remain:

  • Competition: Stripe and Apple Pay are the most important ones. Their TPV continues to grow faster than PayPal's, which is a trend that has to be monitored very closely.
  • Slow growth: The ongoing trend of slowing revenue growth could continue, potentially even leading to revenue declines. In addition, potential growth drivers/efficiency improving measures might not significantly add to the top or bottom line. This would make PayPal a value trap today.
  • Since their valuation is very low and the company is healthy financially, the key risk is opportunity cost. This stock could very well do another few years of producing no significant gains if not perfectly timing the bottoms.

Conclusion

Paypal has had a good run-up after the tariff lows, slightly outperforming the S&P 500. Likewise, its valuation has increased on average by 27% across multiple metrics, making it more expensive. Still, valuation levels are low. Considering all growth initiatives PayPal is trying to incorporate and especially the recently added offline payments option, bears do need some very good reasons as to why they would think PayPal is a Sell here. In fact, PayPal today represents a good risk-to-reward setup but not an excellent one as it was closer to per my last article. Still, it is worth buying today. My price targets are $83 and $92.

data.title: Retirement Yield Trap Or Treasure? 1 Stock To Sell, 1 To Buy
data.description:
When thinking about income-investing in the context of meeting retirement or financial independence objectives, the classical dilemma is choosing between high-yield with higher risk or lower risk with a less attractive yield.

As I have articulated in my previous articles, it certainly makes more sense to take the latter approach, especially if the financial portfolio plays a vital role in covering the future living expenses.

However, it does not necessarily mean that anything that yields 7% and above should be ruled out from the potential investment universe. There are multiple high-yield securities that come with acceptable risk profiles and could be safely included in the retirement income structures. The immediate examples (just to illustrate the point) that come into my mind are: Western Midstream (WES), MPLX LP (MPLX), Virtus InfraCap U.S. Preferred Stock ETF (PFFA), Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (RQI) and Main Street Capital Corporation (MAIN).

Having said that, still, a notable chunk of prudent retirement income-producing securities lie in the 4% to 7% yield range. The probability of a failure is much lower there compared to the 7%+ yield segment.

Another important aspect that is associated with a bit lower yielding securities is the notion of above inflation-level income growth coupled with decent price appreciation prospects. This is because, typically, lower yields are a byproduct of either conservative earnings payout ratios or richer valuations. Conservative payouts mean that a company retains more cash, which could then be invested in incremental growth opportunities. Richer valuations usually indicate strong growth prospects.

These are quite important nuances to consider and appreciate. For example, a retirement or financial independence period could last, say, 20 or 30 years. This is a very long time under which, if the income streams remain constant, the real purchasing power of the collected dividend could deteriorate significantly. So, it would be only prudent to integrate some lower-yielding but stronger growth companies into the equation.

In this context, let me elaborate on two securities. One of them is a bad example and the other one is something, where I would recommend investors consider going long.

The one to avoid: STAG, 4% yield

STAG Industrial, Inc. (STAG) is a pure-play industrial REIT, which holds a portfolio consisting of 597 individual buildings. About 87% of lease exposure lies in the Tier 1 and Tier 2 areas, which is a clear indication of a solid, quality portfolio. These properties are also greatly diversified across 41 states and historically have not registered material vacancies from some client/tenant bankruptcies.

If we look at the financials, then STAG has this aspect nicely covered as well. What I mean by that is that the balance sheet is rated as investment grade, which coupled with CAD payout of 67% renders the case attractive from the income growth perspective. Namely, STAG retains a notable chunk of internal cash generation and has no headwinds stemming from the cost of debt (elevated credit risk premiums) or balance sheet side.

On top of that, STAG enjoys 2.8% annual lease escalators that come from the existing lease agreements. In other words, STAG has growth tailwinds from reinvestment and the embedded organic growth components.

We can see how this has translated into a notable FFO growth per share. In the past five years, the FFO per share has increased by 28%.

Yet, there are two major issues with STAG.

First, since interest rates have gone up, STAG has underperformed and seems to have entered a phase of stagnation.

The issue for STAG just as for most other long-term lease oriented REITs is that they do experience huge pressure on the valuation multiples when the yields are high (and worse, if they are rising). It is hard to see how or why the long-term yields could go down any time soon.

Second, even with all of these reinvestments and FFO growth, the 5-year dividend CAGR has landed at 0.7%, which is completely unacceptable for income investors, who have specifically sacrificed high-yields to access stronger growth. The 10-year dividend CAGR looks the same at 1.1%.

It could very well be so that STAG changes its capital allocation course and starts to focus more on growing dividend payments. But we have to also be aware of that this kind of logic has been there for many years, and nothing has changed.

In a nutshell, there are better ~4% yielding cases for meeting the retirement income portfolio objectives.

The one to buy: SCHD, 3.9% yield

Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) is an income growth focused ETF, which carries a portfolio of 101 U.S. blue-chip businesses that have already achieved robust cash generation levels.

For example, the portfolio-wide ROE is 28%. The realized 5-year earnings growth of these underlying businesses is 9.3%.

SCHD strikes a balance between growth and value. We can see this by combining the aforementioned metrics/details with the portfolio P/CF multiple of 9.5x - i.e., a very reasonable level in the context of ~30% ROE and almost double-digit earnings growth.

Now, if we look at how the dividend payment has evolved, we will see a massive divergence from STAG's case.

Here is a visual representation of this:

SCHD has really managed to deliver on its objective of offering investors an above inflation-level income growth. For example, the 10-year dividend CAGR is 11.3%, 5-year dividend CAGR is 11.4% and 3-year dividend CAGR is 11.2%.

In other words, SCHD has a clear ability to provide attractive dividend growth in a rather durable fashion.

Finally, in my view, what makes the story here extra compelling is SCHD's initial yield of 3.9%. It is already a meaningful level that can move the needle for income investors. And just think of it: by accepting ~20 basis points lower yield than in STAG's case, investors can access ~10% dividend growth potential.

In my view, SCHD is almost a must-have in any income-focused portfolio.

The bottom line

In this article, I have discussed the rationale of including not as high-yielding securities in retirement income focused portfolios. The key advantages are access to above inflation level income growth, price appreciation and diversification from traditional high-yield security exposures (which are inherently concentrated in a small set of sectors).

Plus, I have outlined two security examples, which optically might seem as solid picks for capturing these benefits. I share more detailed arguments why one of them is a clear buy, while the other one is something to skip.

data.title: Suzano: This Is Why The Market Like The JV With Kimberly-Clark
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend buying Suzano (NYSE:SUZ) shares after the announcement of the joint venture with Kimberly-Clark. This article is a continuation of my initial coverage article published on May 19, 2024, with the same recommendation.

In this article, I will provide a detailed analysis of the deal that expands the company's global presence. Most notably, the company diversifies its business without increasing leverage too much, while the valuation presents an opportunity.

Corporate Profile

Suzano is the world's largest pulp producer, with a capacity of 13.4 million tons of kraft pulp. The company is also the largest producer of writing and printing paper in Brazil and the second-largest producer of paperboard.

Below, we will discuss in detail the joint venture between Suzano and Kimberly-Clark, but it is worth noting that if the deal actually goes through after regulatory approvals, Suzano would become the eighth-largest global tissue producer.

The Deal: Joint Venture With Kimberly-Clark

Last week, Suzano announced a proposal for a 51% joint venture with Kimberly-Clark (NASDAQ:KMB) to operate together in the tissue division. Suzano will appoint the CEO and CFO of the operation.

Suzano will pay $1.7 billion for a 51% stake in the JV, and Kimberly-Clark will hold a 49% stake. Suzano will also have the option to buy Kimberly-Clark's stake starting in the third year of operation.

The JV will include 22 mills in 14 countries and more than 9,000 employees. The operation will have a total capacity of 1 million tons of tissue and annual net revenue of $3.3 billion. Suzano estimates an internal rate of return (IRR) of 15.5% for the operation.

The transaction does not include Kimberly-Clark’s U.S. assets or personal care business but will encompass all other international tissue businesses, in addition to professional segments and family care. Below we have the brands.

The market liked the deal, and the shares rose 7.38% on the day the deal was announced. Below, I will give my opinion and impressions on the deal.

My Analysis And Impressions Of The Deal

Kimberly-Clark's assets generated EBITDA of approximately $525 million in 2024, so the transaction multiple is 6.5x EBITDA, above the multiple negotiated by Suzano of 6.08x EBITDA. However, I view the transaction positively.

This JV mitigates the risks of Suzano's aggressive international expansion, in addition to being a major step for the company to increase its exposure to value-added products and reduce its dependence on the pulp cycle. Keeping Kimberly-Clark in the operation is strategic, since it is the company that created the assets and will be able to transfer knowledge to Suzano.

As we have seen, the company will pay $1.7 billion for 51% of the JV. Suzano reported in Q1 2025 a cash position of BRL 16.83 billion ($2.94 billion) and a strong adjusted EBITDA of BRL 4.25 billion ($743 million) in the pulp business alone. Management indicates that no debt issuance will be necessary, and leverage should remain close to the current level.

Overall, I see Suzano expanding its business globally and reducing its earnings exposure to pulp prices. This is done without abruptly increasing leverage and supports my recommendation to buy the shares.

Valuation: Relevant Discount

Suzano and its peers have their net income significantly impacted by exchange rate variations and commodity prices such as pulp and paper. Therefore, the best way to analyze valuation is through the EV/EBITDA multiple, in my opinion, as we can eliminate the aforementioned effects.

As we have seen throughout the article, Suzano is a company with global operations, so I do not see any sense in the disparity between the EV/EBITDA multiple of the sector and the company itself. Suzano is currently trading at 6.08x EBITDA, and the sector average is trading at 8.43x EBITDA.

In my opinion, this difference may reflect Suzano's excessive exposure to pulp prices, while its competitors have more diversified businesses. The market may interpret the JV as a first step for the company to present more stable cash flows and businesses with less volatility, and if this occurs, a repricing of multiples may occur.

If Suzano returns to trading at the sector average, we have an upside of 38% that corroborates the recommendation to buy the shares. Additionally, I feel very confident in the recommendation when I see that Seeking Alpha's powerful Quant tools recommend a strong buy on the stock.

Potential Threats To The Bullish Thesis

As we saw in the chapter "My Analysis And Impressions Of The Deal", although management does not indicate issuing new debt to execute the deal, we have no guarantee that the JV will not leverage itself in the future, especially with Suzano's bold growth history. Additionally, we should remember that Suzano may appoint a CEO and CFO

Another risk is that Kimberly-Clark will transfer global brands such as Kleenex, Scott, Suave, and Scottex to the joint venture for 30 years without paying royalties, however, there will be new licensing negotiations after this period.

Finally, Suzano projects that the closing of the deal should occur within 12 months since several regulatory approvals are required, especially because the deal involves several countries. If the deal is not completed due to some obstacle, investors may be disappointed and sell their shares.

The Bottom Line

In my opinion, the most important objective achieved with the creation of the JV is for Suzano to grow in value-added products and not depend solely on pulp prices, which bring great volatility to results.

The transaction was carried out without compromising Suzano's leverage, which pleased the market. Additionally, the valuation includes an upside of 38% for the shares, which is compelling.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my recommendation to buy Suzano shares. In my view, the negotiation was very well conducted in order to expand the company's business without compromising its finances. This only further highlights the excellent risk/return ratio of the case.

data.title: Why The World's Economic Forecasters Aren't Panicking Over Tariff Wars
data.description:
The global economy will suffer only a little from the current tariff turmoil, according to the average economist forecasting major countries. Back in December, world economic growth had been expected to run 2.8% in 2025 and 2.9% in 2026. As of June 2025, expectations have dropped only to 2.5% and 2.6%.

To understand what professional economists see for the future, FocusEconomics surveys forecasters around the world. Their numbers lead to an average forecast for each significant country, which are then added up for a regional and world forecast. This "bottom-up" approach means that the world forecast FocusEconomics presents is based on how economists on the ground in different countries and regions see those particular countries responding to both internal and external pressures.

The global picture appears brighter than recent headlines suggest, confirming that news sources tend to highlight bad news and negative trends that "might" or "could" lead to problems.

Surprisingly (at least to me), the United States did not pull the world forecast down. Back in December, GDP growth in 2025 and 2026 was predicted to be 1.7% and 2.0% respectively. The June update shows projections of 1.5% and 1.6%. So the U.S. forecast revision was smaller than the world's. China's projected growth rate also dropped less than the world in total. Europe's forecast drop was about equal, in percentage terms, to the world's.

These forecast changes arise not just from President Trump's tariff policies but also everything else in the world. Wars, government policies, demographics and weather all impact the economy, so we cannot really pin all change on the tariff controversy. But the small magnitude of forecast changes illustrates that professional economists are not anticipating disaster, even though they may not be particularly happy with the direction of policy changes. A policy that I consider a mistake may not swing my economic forecast as much as one-tenth of one percent. This is a good reason to avoid rhetoric about how bad or good certain new policies will be.

In most countries, monetary policy is expected by those surveyed by FocusEconomics to ease over the next 18 months. Already in 2025, Canada, the Euro Area and the United Kingdom have cut interest rates. Although this policy assumption will tend to ameliorate GDP deceleration, monetary policy acts with a significant time lag. Further interest rate cuts around the world would have their effects in 2026 and 2027.

Long-term bond rate comparisons currently reflect local monetary policy. So long interest rates have increased in Japan, fallen in the Euro area and remained roughly level in the U.S.

Helping the global economic outlook, oil prices have dropped to $62 per barrel in the United States, down from $80 a year prior. Oil runs a little more expensive in Europe, but the decline in the past 12 months has been comparable to the U.S. Back-of-the-envelope estimates show that the cheaper oil would boost GDP growth by about 0.1 percentage point. Other commodity prices are mostly unchanged.

Rolling all factors together, it makes sense for the economic outlook to have only deteriorated a little. Although the president has imposed some draconian tariffs, he has quickly retreated on many specific levies. Stock market speculators use the term "TACO trade," where the acronym means "Trump Always Chickens Out." If that statement bears truth, then tariffs won't be high for long.

Nonetheless, it's hard to ignore tariff uncertainty as a global factor depressing business hiring and investment and possibly leading consumers to defer discretionary spending. Although the most likely outcome is bad, the range of possibilities is unusually wide at this time.

Business leaders with a global span can take the consensus among economists as comforting. Individual countries will vary, of course. The key takeaway for executives is probably to pay less attention to the news, moderate attention to hard economic data, and lots of attention to their specific business operations.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: REET: Its Past May Not Define Its Future.
data.description:
Geographic diversification has never delivered alpha to global ETFs compared to those focused solely on the US. And that’s a conclusion you reach by comparing an ETF like the iShares Global REITS ETF (NYSEARCA:REET) with other US-only ETFs. Yet, I think that diversification, which has proven to be a real drag on past valuations, could become a “Trojan horse” for REET’s future valuations. Let me explain better.

Some essential information about REET

Built on the FTSE EPRA Nareit Global REITS Net Total Return index, REET is an ETF with nearly $4B in AUM, aiming to provide global exposure to the REITs market. And it does so “in its own way”: it has a very broad “holdings universe,” with 331 positions, yet it maintains a particular focus on the U.S. segment and on the most representative tickers of that market; it also includes about 170 smaller holdings, but they account for less than 8%. The dividend, distributed quarterly, remains competitive, 30-day SEC Yield: 3.39%. But maybe you should know…

The (real) composition of REET

In fact, its globalized composition is somewhat its defining feature, although I prefer to call it “moderately globalized.” That’s because over 70% of it is made up of U.S. stocks, trusts operating on American soil, while the rest is spread across other developed and emerging economies. As for sectors, Retail + Industrial + Healthcare make up nearly 50% of the total. This is often criticized, with many asking: “Where’s 5G? Why only 9% in data centers? That’s the future!” Maybe so, but the largest REITs, with more stable cash flows, are still in retail and industrial. If that changes in the future, those REITs will gradually gain more weight inside REET, and our holdings will adjust accordingly.

Why not a U.S. ETF

As Nareit says in this research: “diversification must be earned.” An interesting thought, because it indirectly criticizes global ETFs; if U.S. REITs, in every time frame from 1990 to today, have shown a better Sharpe ratio than other developed markets and emerging markets, what’s the point of including them in a portfolio? That’s not diversification, it’s dilution of returns, because “in return” you’re not getting any real benefit. And in hindsight, that makes sense.

But is it still true today? Not exactly. Today, there are two variables that investors must deal with, which weren’t there before: currency divergence (the dollar weakening against other developed currencies), and monetary policy divergence (a hawkish Fed versus, for example, a dovish ECB). This generates two different degrees of stress on the real estate market. In the first case, potential margin compression; in the second, expansion.So yes... in the past, REET’s diversification came at a cost, without providing any benefit. But isn’t it the same feeling you get when you buy insurance and the accident never happens?That is, does it make sense in the coming decade to potentially give up a few points of return in exchange for a more neutral portfolio?

Let me explain what I mean.

An unexpected representation

Let me offer this different point of view on analyzing distribution: segmented by monetary policy. What if I told you that 71% of REET’s market cap is exposed to countries with central banks in a hawkish stance, and that less than 25% are clearly in a dovish phase (with 4% neutral)? Maybe at first that doesn’t mean much to you, but perhaps we’re forgetting that:

Retail + Industrial + Healthcare = long duration. So, more sensitive to the trend in real interest rates. That’s why knowing that about 25% is allocated to monetarily accommodative or expansionary markets (Eurozone, Australia, United Kingdom, Japan, Singapore, and Canada) is better than nothing.

In a market characterized by Treasury rates at record levels, a hawkish Fed, a weakening dollar (risk of losing exceptionalism), and inflation at 2.4% still above target, I appreciate the fact that there are long-duration REITs operating in markets where credit spreads are narrowing and inflation is falling; this could potentially support upward prospects for dividends, but also for REIT demand.

RWO: another popular global ETF I dismiss

I identify one in particular: RWO, a global ETF with a similar sector allocation. So, just as I appreciate REET, I also appreciate RWO’s allocation. The difference is that REET is cheaper (RWO has a 0.50% expense ratio), a factor that has led to RWO underperforming REET. Moreover, RWO has fewer holdings, although in terms of market cap this has only a marginal impact.

Risk

I love challenging what I just said, and in doing so I ask myself: “but how can you think REET offers diversification when it has a beta of 0.96 with the S&P 500?”Basically, by including REET in the portfolio, I’m getting something equity-like on the U.S., and one could say: “terrible diversification.” True... in the sense that if the past were a guarantee of the future, then yes, what would be the point of opting for REET? Global markets have lower performance, and apparently they behave like the S&P 500.

But the lever I’ve used to build this article is precisely the attempt to find a solution for an “asymmetry” that could widen in the future between U.S. and foreign markets. In this sense, if non-U.S. REITs were to decouple their performance from the U.S., REET could become a choice with alpha in terms of asset allocation (at least in my opinion).

Conclusion

Let’s take a step back: REET is a “diversified-balanced” solution that I appreciate and consider for a core component of the real estate trust segment in the portfolio. I start from the performance criticism: if U.S. markets have always been more profitable, what real diversification benefit comes from including other markets that are still linked to it? But I make an extra effort and instead ask myself: if the ongoing currency and monetary policy divergence between the U.S. and non-U.S. markets creates a gap compared to post-2008 valuations, what value would this (still not extreme) diversification bring to the portfolio? Well, potentially a positive one. So, in a situation like this, I believe REET deserves a BUY rating.

data.title: Franklin Intermediate Government Bond SMA Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

A stronger-than-expected US economy and sticky US inflation prints led to a hawkish pivot from the US Federal Reserve (Fed), which lowered rates twice during the quarter but signaled only two rate cuts were likely during 2025. Meanwhile, Donald Trump’s presidential election victory in the United States was seen as positive for domestic economic growth but created significant uncertainty elsewhere, as investors worried about the implications of increased US protectionism on global trade. Over the period, US Treasury ('UST') yields moved higher across much of the curve. Very short-dated bonds witnessed declines, while yields increased across longer tenors. Yields rose by 60 basis points ('BPS') to 4.24% on the two-year UST note (US2Y) and by 82 bps to 4.38% on the five-year UST note (US5Y). By the end of the quarter, the yield on the benchmark 10-year UST note (US10Y) moved 79 bps higher to 4.57%.

QUARTERLY KEY PERFORMANCE DRIVERS

Security Selection Allocation Quality
HELPED USTS Underweight USTs with Two Years to Maturity
US Government Agencies
HURT Overweight USTs with Five Years to Maturity
  • Our overall duration exposure was similar to that of the benchmark, however, positioning contributed to returns. Our underweight to the two- year portion of the UST yield curve helped performance over the quarter. In contrast, overweight exposure to USTs with five years to maturity detracted from results.
  • Positioning in US Government Agency was accretive to quarterly performance for the period.

Outlook & Strategy

The US economy strides into 2025 with robust momentum, in contrast to the weaker outlook in other major economies such as Europe and China. At the same time, there is uncertainty surrounding the new US administration’s economic policies. While the tariffs threatened by the new administration would be questionable policy as they could ignite a broader global trade war, they are unlikely to cause major damage to US growth or inflation, in our view. Mass deportations of workers that could adversely affect labor supply seem logistically impractical and therefore perhaps unlikely. Although deregulation and low taxes would promote stronger economic growth, slower disinflation progress could result. The Fed has acknowledged as much as it seems willing to live with above-target inflation for longer rather than jeopardize a robust growth outlook. Both the Fed and market expectations have largely migrated toward our long-held view that the neutral policy rate is around 4%. With the current policy rate at 4.25%-4.5%, the Fed has penciled in only two more cuts for the next 12 months with Chair Powell acknowledging that the policy rate is closer to neutral. Against the backdrop of uncertain policy outcomes, we believe markets should brace for potential bouts of volatility.

Investment Team

Jacob K. Chu, CFAThomas Runkel, CFAAlbert W Chan, CFA

data.title: SkyWest: Don't Miss Takeoff
data.description:
I have been exploring companies that are overlooked by the broader investment community, but which exhibit solid fundamentals, strong operational performance, and clear runway for value creation. These aren’t microcaps or speculative plays—I’m focused on real businesses that are proven operators, yet still trade under the radar.

One such stock is SkyWest, Inc. (NASDAQ:SKYW), a regional airline operator that is very profitable, improved its balance sheet, and continues to generate solid cash flow—all while trading at what, I believe, is a discounted valuation compared to peers.

Airlines have been in flux lately. They recovered from COVID, and then there were pilot shortages. The market seemed to be recovering, and then tariffs and fears of recession arrived. SkyWest has found itself a profitable place in the market. It provides a demanded service and runs its business efficiently to generate good margins.

Company Overview

SkyWest Inc. is the parent company of SkyWest Airlines, the largest regional airline in the U.S. SkyWest provides scheduled passenger flights to destinations in the US, Canada, and Mexico. As of the latest quarterly report, the company offered scheduled passenger and air freight services with approximately 2,350 total daily departures.

SkyWest does not operate and earn the same as the larger main airlines. Substantially all of the company’s flights are operated as United Express, Delta Connection, American Eagle or Alaska Airlines flights under code-share agreements with United Airlines (UAL), Delta Air Lines, Inc. (DAL), American Airlines Group Inc (AAL) or Alaska Air Group (ALK), respectively. Code-share agreements are commercial agreements between airlines that, among other things, allow one airline to use another airline’s flight designator codes on its flights.

Regional airlines primarily earn revenue through long-term, fixed-fee, code share agreements with major carriers—United, Delta, American, and Alaska Airlines. Under the fixed-fee agreements, capacity purchase agreements (CPAs), the major airline partners generally pay SkyWest fixed rates for operating the aircraft primarily based on the number of completed flights, flight time, and number of aircraft under contract. The major airlines either pay directly or reimburse for many of the direct operating costs, including fuel expenses. The other revenue option is a Prorate Agreement. This is where the two airlines negotiate a fare proration formula for certain routes. The regional airline receives a percentage of the ticket revenues for those passengers traveling for one portion of their trip on the regional airline and the other portion of their trip on the major airline. Under this format, the regional airline receives all the ticket revenue on a route solely operated by the regional airline. The regional airline may receive more profits if ticket prices increase and fuel decreases. The regional airline will experience the opposite if the ticket prices decrease and fuel increases. More risk and more reward. During 2024, 87% of the flying agreements were CPAs

SkyWest flies a fleet of over 626 aircraft, with 488 in scheduled service or under active CPAs. The remaining fall under SkyWest Charter or SkyWest Leasing.

SkyWest Leasing earns revenue from financing new aircraft with debt under the capacity purchase agreements. It also includes revenue from leasing aircraft and engines to third parties. SkyWest Charter began operations in 2023. It offers on-demand charter services.

The company’s business model is built on predictable revenue from CPAs, where major airlines pay fixed monthly rates for aircraft operations and ownership, largely insulating SkyWest from demand or fuel price volatility. It is a matter of SkyWest operating its business efficiently to make a profit based upon the fixed rates. This is very much a different business model than a full airline carrier. There is much less risk. There is also less potential upside.

Regional Airlines and SkyWest

There is a good niche market for regional airlines. I myself have flown SkyWest on a shorter flight. I flew out of a small airport, quick to get in and on the flight. It was for a quick business trip, I was there for two days at a conference. I could have driven for 6 hours, or instead I hopped on a flight for 1 hour. It was very affordable and the best way to make a quick trip. This market is not worth it for the larger players to try and manage. The regional airlines can focus on being very cost-efficient. They run smaller planes than the larger airlines. Their pilots are typically new pilots who are paid much less, the regional airlines are feeder programs for the larger airlines. They are able to keep rates low enough that it is worth it to fly, even for shorter distances, rather than drive. I think there will continue to be a market for this business model in the US. Travel is not going away, and the convenience and affordability of it makes it easier than ever.

There has also been a squeeze in the airline space. There are less new airlines entering the market, but also less going out of business. According to the IATA, 28 new airlines started in 2024 and 19 ceased flying. That is the lowest number for both figures in a decade. The market has consolidated and SkyWest has its position established in that market. I see less new bids entering the market for regional contracts. I don’t know if that trend is necessarily good for the consumers, but I see it as a positive for SkyWest.

SkyWest is also the largest regional airline. I believe its size provides it more protection from cancelled contracts with the major players or going under as compared to many peers. It would be difficult for a major airline to replace the flights and revenue coming from SkyWest. I think this provides the company a layer of protection. SkyWest has emerged from the past few years as a leader, with scale, efficiency, and disciplined capital management. We will go through some more detail on this performance throughout the analysis.

Financial Performance

As previously noted, the majority of SkyWest's revenue is based upon fixed-rate contracts. This means there is much less fluctuation in their income and expenses. Their job is to manage their expenses and performance to turn a profit on the fee they are provided. SkyWest has been excelling at that over the past few years. The company has not only been able to grow its capacity but also do so while maintaining its efficiency.

Here’s a look at the recent results:

Q1 2025

Q4 2024

Q3 2024

Q2 2024

Q1 2024

Q4 2023

Source: Seeking Alpha

The company has been able to increase revenues at a double-digit percentage for the past 6 quarters. Revenue growth occurred for all revenue lines, including prorate, CPA, and charter. They have also been increasing their operating margin with their growth. The biggest gains are seen in the EPS. The company does not have a large number of shares outstanding, which means a small gain in profitability can equal large profits per share. The growth in EPS is astounding. Growth is not expected to continue at similar rates going forward, but it shows how the company has been able to execute and be very profitable.

Outlook

The company expects the growth will continue throughout the year as well. Management estimates that block hours (the amount of flight time, and through which they are compensated) will increase 12-13% year over year in 2025. It also estimates 2025 EPS in the low to mid $9 per share range. That would be good for approximately 20% growth in the EPS for the year. Revenue is expected to grow 10% for the year. The growth is expected to slow going into 2026. Revenue is expected to grow just over 5% and EPS to grow another 11.5%. While down from the prior few years, a double-digit growth in earnings is still impressive.

The company announced the delivery of 16 new E175s over the next two years as well. This will increase the flight time available.

Valuation and Peer Comparison

SkyWest is trading around $100 (as of writing). It is the largest regional airline and has been growing at a great pace the past year. It is also very profitable, showing that management is running an efficient business. There are unfortunately not a lot of regional airline carriers that are publicly traded. Many of the regional airlines have been purchased by Delta, United or American. They all operate their own regional airlines through wholly owned subsidiaries. I pulled some comps that are available. Alaska and JetBlue Airways Corporation (JBLU) are similar in size but operate as the main airline more so than operating on a fixed rate agreement. In fact, Alaska has a code sharing agreement with SkyWest. Mesa Air Group (MESA) is a regional airline that is much smaller. Air Canada (OTCQX:ACDVF)is a good comparable company other than obviously being based in Canada. The market will be different in Canada, but the company does have a strong presence in the US. The chart below outlines some metrics between the companies.

P/E

EPS

Revenue YOY

EBIT Margin

Source: Seeking Alpha

SkyWest really separates itself with its profitability. The growth rate is strong, but it leads the pack in profitability. It is the only one on the list with double-digit margins. The EPS is far above any competitor as well. SkyWest is the leader in both growth and margins.

Air Canada has a lower PE ratio but with no growth compared to 20% year over year for SkyWest. This leaves SkyWest as the value play as well. It also has the highest block hour reliability and is the preferred partner for all four major U.S. airlines.

Capital Allocation

The company has been prudent with their capital. The company obviously has large capital asset purchases. It has been using its profits to pay down debt as well as return some to shareholders. The company had $751 million in cash at the end of Q1. They ended the quarter with a debt level of $2.6 billion, down from $2.7 billion the prior quarter. The company plans to continue to deleverage.

SkyWest also purchased 141,000 shares for $14 million during the latest quarter. They also increased the existing share repurchase plan by $250 million, with $22 million remaining from the prior authorization.

Risks

The macro is a large risk. We have seen a lot of volatility with the airlines since COVID. It seems that flight demand was doing great. Then tariffs and other conflicts have brought into question a recession and what that will do to flight demand. This continues to be a large risk for the company. If we enter a recession, there will be less demand for flights and the company could enter a downturn.

One of the largest risks to SkyWest is that the major airlines will move more into the regional flight market. This can be through their own subsidiaries operating as regional airlines. Each of the major airlines operates a wholly owned regional airline. This could also take on the form of legacy airlines flying larger planes to an airport previously served by the regional airlines. Demand is growing faster than new airports are being built. Which means airlines will try to squeeze more seats where they can. They would be able to reduce operating costs per seat using the larger aircraft and avoid paying the fee to regional airlines. If there is enough demand, the airlines could start flying the larger planes with more seats. This would leave less demand for the regional airlines. This has already occurred in one instance with United Airlines. The company recently spent $2 billion renovating its Houston hub; these upgrades will convert 30 hubs that were used for small regional jets to 18 that can accommodate larger aircraft. There has to be enough demand for the airline to choose to make this transition, as their larger aircraft need to be at a high enough capacity to make it worth the change.

The company also has concentration risk, with Delta and United accounting for over two-thirds of aircraft under contract. These contracts are long term, so the short-term risk is not as high, but there is risk that these companies choose to reduce the amount they contract with SkyWest.

Labor costs are a major factor for the company. If there is a pilot shortage, as we saw during COVID, then the company will have a much harder time finding pilots. This will increase costs and also bring down block hours, making it much more difficult to turn a profit.

Conclusion

SkyWest has done a remarkable job recovering from post-pandemic challenges. They’ve stabilized margins and grown revenues. They run efficiently, and their profitability has flown past the competition. The company has been able to grow revenues at a double-digit rate and expects to do the same next year as well. The company has a low valuation with a P/E ratio that does not reflect this growth. The worries of recession have not affected SkyWest so far, as it has not seen any slowdown; in fact, they expect to grow the remainder of the year.

The regional airlines, SkyWest included, operate on a much different model than a traditional airline. This model provides more consistent income and allows them to profit if they can run efficiently. There are some pros and cons to this business model. The largest risk is that the larger airlines will choose to in-house this market rather than contract with regional airlines, such as SkyWest. I think the company is partly insulated from this risk.

With reliable contracts, strong free cash flow, and expected continued growth, I believe SKYW remains underappreciated. I do not yet own any shares of the stock, but will be looking to open a position.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The U.K.'s Deteriorating Jobs Market Should Ring Alarm Bells At The Bank Of England
data.description:
By James Smith

Job losses appear to be picking up pace

A lot has happened in the UK over the past month, but a surprisingly bad jobs report has set off fresh economic alarm bells recently. It showed that employee numbers are falling at the fastest rate, outside of the Covid-19 pandemic, since the data began in 2014. These numbers tend to get revised up later, but still, employment is down by more than a percentage point since the end of last year and has fallen in nine out of the last 10 months.

At face value, this has a clear recessionary feel to it. We’re sceptical that’s where we’re heading, but these figures do at least tell us that the economy will struggle to sustain the astonishingly strong 0.7% quarterly growth rate recorded in the first quarter.

In fact, we think those figures probably overstate recent strength. Some of it was linked to firms' frontloading of US tariffs. But in general, the GDP data has been stronger in the first half of the year than the second, ever since 2022. That hints at problems with the way the numbers are adjusted for seasonal patterns.

Weaker growth and a cooler jobs market raise questions about the Bank of England’s reticence to cut interest rates more quickly. Wage growth has already slowed from 6% to 5% in annual terms, and we think it should fall towards 4% and perhaps below by the end of the year. Similarly, we think services inflation – which is intrinsically linked to wages and a key focus at the Bank of England – should fall from 5.4% to 4% by early 2026.

The bar for the Bank to cut rates more rapidly appears set relatively high, judging by recent comments. But we expect the committee to keep lowering them once per quarter through this year, in August and November, with further cuts in 2026. Were the cooling in the jobs market to gather pace – in particular, if redundancies were to increase off their current low levels – then that could be a catalyst to move more rapidly.

Private sector employee numbers are falling

A weaker jobs market is bad news for the Treasury

A weaker jobs market is yet another headache for Chancellor Rachel Reeves. Even before the jobs numbers turned south, we thought it was highly likely the Office for Budget Responsibility would be forced to revise down its growth forecasts. That's on account of tariff uncertainty and weaker productivity growth. That would lower projections for tax revenues and make it much harder to balance the budget later this decade, which is what the fiscal rules require.

In March, the government was forecast to generate a slim surplus later this decade. By the autumn, we think those forecasts will show a deficit, particularly considering the growing pressure on the Treasury to boost spending. The Chancellor's spending review, which allocates previously set spending pots between government departments, demonstrated that there isn’t enough money to go around. Day-to-day spending will likely have to rise more quickly in 2026 than currently budgeted.

That leaves a shortfall, perhaps above £20bn, that'll need to be filled to make the fiscal rules add up. We think tax rises are therefore inevitable and the way the fiscal rules work means that, counterintuitively, a weaker jobs market only makes that more likely.
